questions.question: Choose the option nearest in meaning to the underlined word: The king was known for his benevolent disposition towards the poor.
questions.explanation: 'Benevolent' means well-meaning and kindly; 'charitable' is the closest in meaning.

questions.question: Choose the option that is opposite in meaning to the underlined word: The chairman's verbose speech bored the audience.
questions.explanation: 'Verbose' means using more words than necessary; its antonym is 'concise', meaning brief and clear.

questions.question: Choose the option that best completes the sentence: The jury ______ the accused after hearing all the evidence.
questions.explanation: 'Acquitted' means to formally declare someone not guilty, which best suits the context of hearing evidence.

questions.question: Choose the option nearest in meaning to the underlined word: The tedious journey lasted for twelve hours.
questions.explanation: 'Tedious' means tiringly long and dull; 'monotonous' means lacking variety and boring, which is the nearest meaning.

questions.question: Choose the option that best fills the gap: She was so ______ that she refused to change her mind despite all the evidence.
questions.explanation: 'Obstinate' means stubbornly refusing to change one's opinion, which fits the context.

questions.question: Choose the option nearest in meaning to the underlined word: The garrulous old man talked endlessly about his exploits.
questions.explanation: 'Garrulous' means excessively talkative, especially on trivial matters.

questions.question: Choose the option that is opposite in meaning to the underlined word: The reticent student rarely participated in class discussions.
questions.explanation: 'Reticent' means not revealing one's thoughts or feelings readily; its antonym is 'communicative'.

questions.question: Choose the option that best completes the sentence: The scientist made a ______ discovery that changed the course of medicine.
questions.explanation: A 'landmark' discovery is one that marks an important stage or turning point, fitting the context of changing medicine.

questions.question: Choose the option nearest in meaning to the underlined word: He was so frugal that he never wasted a kobo.
questions.explanation: 'Frugal' means sparing or economical with money; 'thrifty' is the nearest synonym.

questions.question: Choose the option that is opposite in meaning to the underlined word: The magnanimous leader forgave his enemies.
questions.explanation: 'Magnanimous' means generous or forgiving; its antonym is 'petty', meaning small-minded or ungenerous.

questions.question: Choose the option that best completes the sentence: The students were ______ for their excellent performance in the examination.
questions.explanation: 'Commended' means praised or recommended, which fits a context of excellent performance.

questions.question: Choose the option nearest in meaning to the underlined word: Her lucid explanation helped everyone understand the concept.
questions.explanation: 'Lucid' means expressed clearly and easy to understand.

questions.question: Choose the option nearest in meaning to the underlined word: The politician was surrounded by sycophants who praised his every move.
questions.explanation: 'Sycophants' are people who act obsequiously toward someone in order to gain advantage; 'flatterers' is the nearest meaning.

questions.question: Choose the option that best completes the sentence: The new policy was met with ______ from the opposition party.
questions.explanation: Opposition parties typically counter new government policies, so 'resistance' is the most appropriate word.

questions.question: Choose the option nearest in meaning to the underlined word: The government promised to ameliorate the conditions in the rural areas.
questions.explanation: 'Ameliorate' means to make something bad better; 'improve' is the nearest synonym.

questions.question: Choose the option that is opposite in meaning to the underlined word: The diligent student always completed her assignments on time.
questions.explanation: 'Diligent' means careful and persistent; its antonym is 'lazy'.

questions.question: Choose the option that best completes the sentence: The old bridge was ______ because it could no longer bear heavy loads.
questions.explanation: A bridge that can no longer bear loads would be 'condemned', meaning officially declared unfit for use.

questions.question: Choose the option nearest in meaning to the underlined word: Please make only pertinent comments during the meeting.
questions.explanation: 'Pertinent' means relevant to the matter at hand; 'relevant' is its synonym.

questions.question: Choose the option that is opposite in meaning to the underlined word: Amaka is a gregarious person who enjoys large gatherings.
questions.explanation: 'Gregarious' means fond of company; its antonym is 'introverted', meaning preferring one's own company.

questions.question: Choose the option that best completes the sentence: The doctor advised her to ______ from strenuous activities after the surgery.
questions.explanation: 'Abstain' means to refrain from something, fitting the medical advice to avoid strenuous activity.

questions.question: Choose the option nearest in meaning to the underlined word: The divorce proceedings were acrimonious and lasted for years.
questions.explanation: 'Acrimonious' means angry and bitter, especially in disputes.

questions.question: Choose the option that is opposite in meaning to the underlined word: The loquacious woman talked throughout the flight.
questions.explanation: 'Loquacious' means tending to talk a great deal; its antonym is 'quiet'.

questions.question: Choose the option that best completes the sentence: Despite the hardship, she remained ______ and never gave up.
questions.explanation: 'Resilient' means able to recover quickly from difficulties, fitting someone who does not give up.

questions.question: Choose the option nearest in meaning to the underlined word: The government set up a scheme to help indigent farmers.
questions.explanation: 'Indigent' means poor and needy.

questions.question: Choose the option that is opposite in meaning to the underlined word: The taciturn judge rarely commented on cases outside the courtroom.
questions.explanation: 'Taciturn' means reserved or uncommunicative; its antonym is 'talkative'.

questions.question: Choose the option that best completes the sentence: The two countries signed a ______ agreement to end the conflict.
questions.explanation: 'Bilateral' means involving two parties or countries, fitting an agreement between two nations.

questions.question: Choose the option nearest in meaning to the underlined word: The indefatigable activist campaigned for human rights for decades.
questions.explanation: 'Indefatigable' means persisting tirelessly; 'tireless' is the correct synonym.

questions.question: Choose the option that is opposite in meaning to the underlined word: The parsimonious landlord refused to repair the leaking roof.
questions.explanation: 'Parsimonious' means excessively unwilling to spend money; its antonym is 'generous'.

questions.question: Choose the option that best fills the gap: His ______ behavior at the dinner table embarrassed his parents.
questions.explanation: 'Uncouth' means lacking good manners or refinement, which would cause embarrassment.

questions.question: Choose the option nearest in meaning to the underlined word: The ostentatious display of wealth angered the poor villagers.
questions.explanation: 'Ostentatious' means characterized by a vulgar display of wealth; 'showy' is the nearest synonym.

questions.question: Choose the option that is opposite in meaning to the underlined word: Fame can be ephemeral if not supported by real talent.
questions.explanation: 'Ephemeral' means lasting for a very short time; its antonym is 'permanent'.

questions.question: Choose the option that best completes the sentence: The journalist was accused of ______ the facts in his report.
questions.explanation: Being 'accused' implies wrongdoing; 'distorting' means misrepresenting facts, which fits an accusation.

questions.question: Choose the option nearest in meaning to the underlined word: The new teacher was aloof and rarely interacted with students informally.
questions.explanation: 'Aloof' means not friendly or forthcoming; emotionally distant.

questions.question: Choose the option that is opposite in meaning to the underlined word: I appreciate his candid assessment of the situation.
questions.explanation: 'Candid' means truthful and straightforward; its antonym is 'evasive', meaning deliberately vague.

questions.question: Choose the option that best fills the gap: The team's ______ performance in the finals disappointed their supporters.
questions.explanation: 'Mediocre' means of only average quality; a mediocre performance would cause disappointment.

questions.question: Choose the option nearest in meaning to the underlined word: She sat by the window with a pensive look on her face.
questions.explanation: 'Pensive' means engaged in deep or serious thought.

questions.question: Choose the option that is opposite in meaning to the underlined word: They reached an amicable settlement after months of negotiation.
questions.explanation: 'Amicable' means having a spirit of friendliness; its antonym is 'hostile'.

questions.question: Choose the option that best completes the sentence: The government decided to ______ the old law and replace it with a new one.
questions.explanation: 'Repeal' means to revoke or annul a law, which matches 'replacing it with a new one'.

questions.question: Choose the option nearest in meaning to the underlined word: She is a zealous worker who always goes the extra mile.
questions.explanation: 'Zealous' means showing great energy or enthusiasm; 'enthusiastic' is the nearest synonym.

questions.question: Choose the option that is opposite in meaning to the underlined word: It was prudent of him to save some money for emergencies.
questions.explanation: 'Prudent' means acting with care and thought for the future; its antonym is 'reckless'.

questions.question: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence: Each of the boys ______ expected to bring his own textbook.
questions.explanation: 'Each' is a singular indefinite pronoun and takes a singular verb 'is'.

questions.question: Choose the correct option to complete the sentence: By the time he arrived, she ______ for three hours.
questions.explanation: The past perfect continuous 'had been waiting' is used to show an action that was ongoing before another past action ('arrived').

questions.question: Choose the correct option: Between you and ______, I think the plan will fail.
questions.explanation: The preposition 'between' takes an object pronoun. 'Me' is the correct object form, not 'I' or 'myself'.

questions.question: Choose the correct preposition: He is allergic ______ dust.
questions.explanation: The correct collocation is 'allergic to'. 'To' is the required preposition here.

questions.question: Choose the correct option: The jury ______ divided in their opinion.
questions.explanation: When a collective noun like 'jury' acts as individuals with different opinions, a plural verb is used.

questions.question: Choose the correct option: She ______ her homework before her mother came home.
questions.explanation: The past perfect 'had done' is used for an action completed before another past action ('came home').

questions.question: Choose the grammatically correct option: The winner of the competition was ______.
questions.explanation: After the linking verb 'was', a subject pronoun (nominative case) is required. 'She' is the correct form.

questions.question: Choose the correct conjunction: He worked very hard ______ he did not pass the examination.
questions.explanation: 'Yet' is used as a conjunction to show contrast between two ideas that seem contradictory.

questions.question: Choose the correct option: Neither the students nor the teacher ______ present at the meeting.
questions.explanation: With 'neither...nor', the verb agrees with the subject nearest to it. 'Teacher' is singular, so 'is' is correct.

questions.question: Choose the correct article: He is ______ honest man.
questions.explanation: 'An' is used before words beginning with a vowel sound. 'Honest' begins with a silent 'h', so the vowel sound /ɒ/ is heard.

questions.question: Choose the correct option: If I were rich, I ______ build a hospital in my village.
questions.explanation: In second conditional sentences (hypothetical situations), 'would' is used in the main clause.

questions.question: Choose the correct passive form: The secretary ______ the letter yesterday.
questions.explanation: 'Yesterday' indicates past tense. The correct passive form in simple past is 'was typed by'.

questions.question: Choose the correct preposition: The minister was accused ______ corruption.
questions.explanation: The correct collocation is 'accused of'. 'Of' is the required preposition.

questions.question: Choose the correct option: A number of students ______ absent yesterday.
questions.explanation: 'A number of' is followed by a plural noun and takes a plural verb. Compare 'the number of students was'.

questions.question: Choose the correct tense: I ______ in this city for ten years now.
questions.explanation: Present perfect continuous is used for an action that started in the past and continues to the present ('for ten years now').

questions.question: He said, 'I will come tomorrow.' In reported speech, this becomes: He said that he ______ the following day.
questions.explanation: In reported speech, 'will' changes to 'would', and 'tomorrow' becomes 'the following day'.

questions.question: Choose the correct preposition: She is married ______ a doctor.
questions.explanation: The correct collocation is 'married to'. One is married 'to' another person.

questions.question: Choose the correct option: The news ______ shocking.
questions.explanation: 'News' is an uncountable noun that always takes a singular verb. 'Was' is correct.

questions.question: Choose the correct option: Everyone must bring ______ own lunch to the picnic.
questions.explanation: In formal usage, 'his' is used as the generic singular pronoun to refer back to 'everyone'.

questions.question: Choose the correct option: He plays ______ football every evening.
questions.explanation: No article is used before the names of sports and games.

questions.question: Choose the correct option: He suggested that we ______ early.
questions.explanation: After 'suggest that', the subjunctive mood requires the base form of the verb (bare infinitive), hence 'leave'.

questions.question: Choose the correct relative pronoun: The man ______ car was stolen reported to the police.
questions.explanation: 'Whose' is the possessive relative pronoun, used here to show that the car belongs to the man.

questions.question: Choose the correct option: She ______ to Lagos before the rain started.
questions.explanation: Past perfect 'had gone' shows an action completed before another past action ('before the rain started').

questions.question: Choose the correct preposition: He died ______ malaria.
questions.explanation: One dies 'of' a disease. 'Die of' is the correct collocation.

questions.question: Choose the correct option: The committee ______ yet to reach a decision.
questions.explanation: When a collective noun like 'committee' acts as a single unit, a singular verb is used.

questions.question: Choose the correct option: She ______ in the competition and won first prize.
questions.explanation: An adverb modifies a verb; 'well' (adverb) not 'good' (adjective) modifies the verb 'sang'. Past tense is needed.

questions.question: Choose the correct question tag: She is your sister, ______?
questions.explanation: The question tag for a positive statement with 'she is' must be negative 'isn't she'.

questions.question: Choose the correct preposition: He was punished ______ his misbehavior.
questions.explanation: The correct collocation is 'punished for' — one is punished 'for' an offense.

questions.question: Choose the correct tense: When I ______ home, my mother was cooking.
questions.explanation: Simple past 'reached' is used for an action that interrupted another ongoing past action ('was cooking').

questions.question: Choose the correct option: He enjoys ______ football with his friends.
questions.explanation: The verb 'enjoy' is always followed by a gerund (-ing form).

questions.question: Choose the correct option: Either the manager or his assistants ______ to blame.
questions.explanation: With 'either...or', the verb agrees with the nearest subject. 'Assistants' is plural, so 'are' is correct.

questions.question: Choose the correct preposition: His work is different ______ what I expected.
questions.explanation: The correct collocation is 'different from'. This is the standard British English usage.

questions.question: Choose the correct option (subjunctive): The doctor recommended that she ______ in bed for a week.
questions.explanation: After 'recommend that', the subjunctive mood requires the base form of the verb, hence 'stay' not 'stays'.

questions.question: Choose the correct option: The principal, together with all the teachers, ______ the students at the assembly.
questions.explanation: When a phrase like 'together with' comes between the subject and verb, the verb agrees with the main subject 'principal' (singular). Past tense 'addressed' is appropriate in narrative context.

questions.question: Choose the correct option: She asked him ______ he had been all day.
questions.explanation: 'Where' is used to ask about place or location; 'where he had been' refers to his location.

questions.question: Which of the following words has the stress on the second syllable?
questions.explanation: 'Mistake' is stressed on the second syllable: mis-TAKE. The others are stressed on the first syllable.

questions.question: Which of the following words contains a silent consonant?
questions.explanation: In 'know', the letter 'k' is silent. The word is pronounced /noʊ/.

questions.question: Which pair of words contains the same vowel sound?
questions.explanation: Both 'gun' /ɡʌn/ and 'fun' /fʌn/ contain the vowel sound /ʌ/.

questions.question: Which of these words rhymes with 'great'?
questions.explanation: 'Great' is pronounced /ɡreɪt/ and 'wait' is pronounced /weɪt/. Both end with the /eɪt/ sound.

questions.question: In which of the following words is 'ch' pronounced as /k/?
questions.explanation: In 'chorus', the 'ch' is pronounced /k/ as in /ˈkɔːrəs/. This is common in Greek-derived words.

questions.question: Which of the following words has the primary stress on the first syllable?
questions.explanation: 'Open' is stressed on the first syllable: O-pen. The others are stressed on the second syllable.

questions.question: How many syllables does the word 'photography' have?
questions.explanation: 'Photography' has four syllables: pho-tog-ra-phy.

questions.question: Which of the following words contains the vowel sound /ɔː/?
questions.explanation: 'Caught' /kɔːt/ contains the long vowel sound /ɔː/. The others contain short vowel sounds.

questions.question: Which of the following words has a silent consonant?
questions.explanation: In 'write', the 'w' is silent. The word is pronounced /raɪt/.

questions.question: In the word 'education', where does the primary stress fall?
questions.explanation: 'Education' is stressed on the third syllable: e-du-CA-tion /ˌedʒʊˈkeɪʃən/.

questions.question: Which of the following words rhymes with 'through'?
questions.explanation: 'Through' is pronounced /θruː/ and 'drew' is pronounced /druː/. Both end with the /uː/ sound.

questions.question: Which pair of words contains the same initial consonant sound?
questions.explanation: 'Phone' /foʊn/ and 'fine' /faɪn/ both begin with the /f/ sound. 'Ph' in 'phone' represents /f/.

questions.question: In which of the following words is 'ough' pronounced differently from the others?
questions.explanation: In 'tough', 'rough', and 'enough', 'ough' is pronounced /ʌf/. In 'through', 'ough' is pronounced /uː/.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct stress pattern for the word 'economy'?
questions.explanation: 'Economy' /ɪˈkɒnəmi/ is stressed on the second syllable: e-CON-o-my.

questions.question: Which of the following words has the same vowel sound as 'bird'?
questions.explanation: 'Bird' /bɜːd/ and 'heard' /hɜːd/ both contain the vowel sound /ɜː/.

questions.question: In which of the following sentences is the word 'present' used as a verb?

options.A: She received a present from her father.
options.B: He will present his findings tomorrow.

questions.explanation: In option B, 'present' functions as a verb meaning to show/demonstrate. As a verb, it is stressed on the second syllable: pre-SENT.

questions.question: Which of the following words contains the consonant cluster /str/?
questions.explanation: 'Strap' begins with the consonant cluster /str/. The other words do not contain this cluster.

questions.question: Which of the following words has the stress on the last syllable?
questions.explanation: 'Machine' /məˈʃiːn/ is stressed on the last syllable: ma-CHINE.

questions.question: Which of the following words has the same vowel sound as 'fat'?
questions.explanation: 'Fat' /fæt/ and 'flat' /flæt/ both contain the short vowel sound /æ/.

questions.question: Which word is stressed on the second syllable when used as a noun?
questions.explanation: These are nouns stressed on the first syllable. However, 'record' as a verb is stressed on the second syllable: re-CORD. The others follow the same noun/verb stress shift pattern. Wait — as a noun, REcord is stressed on the first syllable. This tests that knowledge.

questions.question: How many syllables does the word 'beautiful' have?
questions.explanation: 'Beautiful' has three syllables: beau-ti-ful.

questions.question: The word 'certificate' is correctly stressed on which syllable?
questions.explanation: 'Certificate' /səˈtɪfɪkət/ carries the primary stress on the second syllable: cer-TIF-i-cate.

questions.question: Which of the following words has a different vowel sound from the others?
questions.explanation: 'Bite', 'kite', and 'white' all contain the diphthong /aɪ/. 'Kit' contains the short vowel /ɪ/.

questions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the word 'gnaw'?
questions.explanation: In 'gnaw' /nɔː/, the initial 'g' is silent. The word begins with the /n/ sound.

questions.question: Which of the following words has the stress on the third syllable?
questions.explanation: 'Understand' /ˌʌndəˈstænd/ carries its primary stress on the third syllable: un-der-STAND.

questions.question: To 'beat about the bush' means to:

options.A: look for something in a forest
options.B: avoid coming to the main point

questions.explanation: 'To beat about the bush' means to discuss a matter without getting to the main point.

questions.question: To 'bite the bullet' means to:
questions.explanation: 'Bite the bullet' means to endure a painful or difficult situation that is unavoidable.

questions.question: When someone 'burns the midnight oil', it means:

options.B: working or studying very late into the night

questions.explanation: 'Burn the midnight oil' means to work or study late into the night.

questions.question: To 'turn a blind eye' to something means to:
questions.explanation: 'Turn a blind eye' means to deliberately ignore or overlook something.

questions.question: The expression 'once in a blue moon' means:
questions.explanation: 'Once in a blue moon' means something that happens very infrequently or rarely.

questions.question: To 'let the cat out of the bag' means to:
questions.explanation: 'Let the cat out of the bag' means to accidentally reveal something that was meant to be kept secret.

questions.question: When someone is described as 'a dark horse', it means they are:

options.A: a person with a dark complexion
options.C: a person whose abilities are not widely known but who may surprise others

questions.explanation: 'A dark horse' refers to a person who is little known but unexpectedly succeeds or may succeed.

questions.question: To 'get cold feet' means to:

options.B: become nervous or afraid about something

questions.explanation: 'Get cold feet' means to suddenly become nervous, afraid, or hesitant about doing something.

questions.question: The expression 'spill the beans' means to:
questions.explanation: 'Spill the beans' means to reveal secret or confidential information inadvertently.

questions.question: To 'sit on the fence' means to:

options.A: refuse to make a decision or commitment
options.B: watch a game from the sidelines
options.D: stay in one place for a long time

questions.explanation: 'Sit on the fence' means to remain neutral and avoid taking sides in a dispute or making a decision.

questions.question: When something 'costs an arm and a leg', it means:

options.D: it involves a trade of goods

questions.explanation: 'Cost an arm and a leg' means to be very expensive.

questions.question: To 'go back to the drawing board' means to:

options.C: start planning something again from the very beginning

questions.explanation: 'Go back to the drawing board' means to start over after a plan has failed.

questions.question: The phrase 'at the drop of a hat' means:
questions.explanation: 'At the drop of a hat' means immediately, without needing any persuasion.

questions.question: To 'have a chip on one's shoulder' means to:

options.C: be resentful or feeling aggrieved about something

questions.explanation: 'Have a chip on one's shoulder' means to be sensitive and easily angered due to a feeling of inferiority or resentment.

questions.question: To 'pull someone's leg' means to:
questions.explanation: 'Pull someone's leg' means to tease someone by telling them something untrue as a joke.

questions.question: The expression 'the ball is in your court' means:

options.A: a sports event is currently happening
options.B: it is your turn or responsibility to take the next action
options.C: you have already lost the competition

questions.explanation: 'The ball is in your court' means it is now up to you to take the next step or make a decision.

questions.question: To 'bite the hand that feeds you' means to:

options.B: harm or show ingratitude to a benefactor

questions.explanation: 'Bite the hand that feeds you' means to act ungratefully toward someone who has helped you.

questions.question: When someone 'kicks the bucket', it means:
questions.explanation: 'Kick the bucket' is an informal idiom meaning to die.

questions.question: To 'throw in the towel' means to:
questions.explanation: 'Throw in the towel' means to admit defeat and give up, originating from boxing.

questions.question: The phrase 'a blessing in disguise' means:

options.B: something that seems bad but turns out to have good results

questions.explanation: 'A blessing in disguise' refers to something that seems harmful or unpleasant but eventually turns out to be beneficial.

questions.question: To 'break the ice' means to:

options.B: initiate conversation in a social situation to ease tension

questions.explanation: 'Break the ice' means to do or say something to relieve tension in a social situation.

questions.question: To 'add fuel to the fire' means to:

options.B: make a bad situation considerably worse

questions.explanation: 'Add fuel to the fire' means to worsen an already bad or volatile situation.

questions.question: The expression 'to be in hot water' means to:

options.A: to be swimming in warm water
options.C: to be in serious trouble or difficulty

questions.explanation: 'In hot water' means in a difficult situation that may result in punishment or trouble.

questions.question: To 'hit the nail on the head' means to:

options.B: to be exactly right about something

questions.explanation: 'Hit the nail on the head' means to describe something exactly right or to do something in the most effective way.

questions.question: When someone 'has their head in the clouds', it means they are:
questions.explanation: 'Head in the clouds' means to be out of touch with reality, unrealistic, or given to daydreaming.

questions.question: Identify the figure of speech in: 'The moon is a ghostly galleon tossed upon cloudy seas.'
questions.explanation: A metaphor makes a direct comparison without using 'like' or 'as'. The moon is directly described as a 'galleon'.

questions.question: What figure of speech is used in: 'Life is a journey with no road map.'?
questions.explanation: Life is directly equated to a journey without using 'like' or 'as', making it a metaphor.

questions.question: Identify the figure of speech: 'The stars danced in the night sky.'
questions.explanation: Stars are given the human ability to dance, which is an example of personification.

questions.question: What figure of speech is used in: 'He ran as fast as a cheetah.'?
questions.explanation: The comparison uses 'as...as', making it a simile — a figure of speech comparing two unlike things.

questions.question: Identify the figure of speech: 'I am so hungry I could eat a horse.'
questions.explanation: Hyperbole is a deliberate exaggeration for emphasis. Claiming one could eat a horse is a clear exaggeration.

questions.question: What figure of speech is demonstrated in: 'Peter Piper picked a peck of pickled peppers.'?
questions.explanation: Alliteration is the repetition of the same initial consonant sound. The /p/ sound is repeated throughout.

questions.question: Identify the figure of speech: 'The thunder roared and the lightning screamed.'
questions.explanation: Thunder and lightning are given human abilities (roaring and screaming), which is personification.

questions.question: What figure of speech is illustrated in: 'The bees buzzed loudly in the garden.'?
questions.explanation: Onomatopoeia refers to words that imitate the sounds they describe. 'Buzzed' imitates the sound of bees.

questions.question: Identify the figure of speech: 'He is a lion in battle.'
questions.explanation: He is directly equated to a lion without 'like' or 'as', making it a metaphor.

questions.question: The line 'O Moon! O Sun! Look down on me' is an example of which figure of speech?
questions.explanation: Apostrophe is the figure of speech that involves directly addressing an absent, dead, or non-human entity.

questions.question: What figure of speech is: 'The news was a dagger to his heart.'?
questions.explanation: The news is directly compared to a dagger without using 'like' or 'as', making it a metaphor.

questions.question: Identify the figure of speech: 'It was the best of times, it was the worst of times.'
questions.explanation: Antithesis involves placing contrasting ideas in parallel structures. 'Best' is contrasted with 'worst'.

questions.question: What figure of speech is illustrated by the phrase 'deafening silence'?
questions.explanation: An oxymoron combines two contradictory terms. 'Deafening' (very loud) and 'silence' (complete quiet) are contradictory.

questions.question: The statement 'He is the black sheep of the family' is an example of:
questions.explanation: 'Black sheep' is used as a direct comparison to describe someone who is a disgrace to their group, making it a metaphor.

questions.question: 'The pen is mightier than the sword' is an example of:
questions.explanation: The pen and sword are used metaphorically to represent writing/communication and military force respectively.

questions.question: What figure of speech is: 'She sells seashells by the seashore'?
questions.explanation: Alliteration involves the repetition of initial consonant sounds. Here, the /s/ sound is prominently repeated.

questions.question: Identify the figure of speech: 'The wind whispered through the trees.'
questions.explanation: The wind is given the human quality of whispering, which is personification.

questions.question: What is the figure of speech in: 'He has a heart of stone'?
questions.explanation: His heart is directly equated to stone (meaning he is unfeeling) without 'like' or 'as' — a metaphor.

questions.question: The phrase 'crystal clear' is best described as:
questions.explanation: Alliteration involves repetition of the same initial consonant sound. Both 'crystal' and 'clear' begin with the /k/ sound.

questions.question: What figure of speech is: 'He is as brave as a lion'?
questions.explanation: The comparison uses 'as...as', making it a simile.

questions.question: What does the phrasal verb 'break down' mean in: 'The car broke down on the highway'?
questions.explanation: 'Break down' means to stop functioning; when a vehicle breaks down, it stops working.

questions.question: The phrasal verb 'give up' means:

options.B: to move to a higher position

questions.explanation: 'Give up' means to cease making an effort; to abandon or quit.

questions.question: In the sentence 'They called off the match', 'called off' means:
questions.explanation: 'Call off' means to cancel a planned event.

questions.question: 'Look into' as a phrasal verb means:
questions.explanation: 'Look into' means to investigate or examine a matter.

questions.question: The phrasal verb 'run out of' means:

options.B: to exhaust the supply of something
options.C: to run a very long distance

questions.explanation: 'Run out of' means to use up all of a supply and have none left.

questions.question: In 'She turned down the job offer', 'turned down' means:
questions.explanation: 'Turn down' means to decline or reject an offer.

questions.question: The phrasal verb 'put off' means:
questions.explanation: 'Put off' means to postpone or delay something to a later time.

questions.question: 'Come across' as a phrasal verb means:

options.B: to meet or find by chance

questions.explanation: 'Come across' means to find or meet someone or something unexpectedly.

questions.question: In 'He brought up an interesting point during the meeting', 'brought up' means:
questions.explanation: 'Bring up' in the context of a conversation means to mention or introduce a topic.

questions.question: The phrasal verb 'set up' means:
questions.explanation: 'Set up' means to establish, arrange, or prepare something.

questions.question: In 'The thief got away with the crime', 'got away with' means:
questions.explanation: 'Get away with' means to escape punishment or criticism for doing something wrong.

questions.question: 'Take after' as a phrasal verb means:

options.B: to resemble a parent or relative

questions.explanation: 'Take after' means to resemble or have similar characteristics to a parent or older relative.

questions.question: The phrasal verb 'fall out' means:

options.A: to fall down from a height
options.B: to quarrel or have a disagreement

questions.explanation: 'Fall out' means to have a quarrel or disagreement with someone.

questions.question: In 'She made up a story to explain her absence', 'made up' means:
questions.explanation: 'Make up' means to invent a story or explanation that is not true.

questions.question: 'Go through' as a phrasal verb means:

options.A: to physically pass through a narrow space

questions.explanation: 'Go through' means either to experience something difficult or to examine something carefully.

questions.question: The phrasal verb 'take off' in aviation means:

options.C: to leave the ground and begin flying

questions.explanation: In aviation, 'take off' means when an aircraft leaves the ground and becomes airborne.

questions.question: In 'He backed down from the argument', 'backed down' means:

options.B: withdrew from a position or claim

questions.explanation: 'Back down' means to withdraw from a position, argument, or confrontation.

questions.question: 'Bring about' as a phrasal verb means:
questions.explanation: 'Bring about' means to cause something to happen or to produce a particular result.

questions.question: The phrasal verb 'hold on' most commonly means:
questions.explanation: 'Hold on' most commonly means to wait, as in 'Hold on, I'll be with you in a minute'.

questions.question: In 'She put up with his bad behavior for years', 'put up with' means:
questions.explanation: 'Put up with' means to tolerate something unpleasant or difficult without complaining.

questions.question: What does the proverb 'The early bird catches the worm' mean?

options.A: Birds hunt worms early in the morning
options.B: Those who start early have an advantage over others
options.C: Birds are most active at night
options.D: Early morning is the most dangerous time of day

questions.explanation: This proverb means that people who begin early or act promptly are more likely to succeed.

questions.question: Interpret the saying: 'Don't count your chickens before they are hatched.'

options.A: Learn how to count farm animals
options.B: Don't make plans based on something that has not yet happened
options.C: Chickens take a long time to hatch
options.D: It is important to count animals regularly

questions.explanation: This saying warns against making assumptions or plans based on things that are not yet certain.

questions.question: What does 'A stitch in time saves nine' mean?

options.A: Sewing and tailoring are important skills
options.B: Addressing a problem early prevents it from becoming much worse
options.C: Nine is an important number in sewing

questions.explanation: This proverb advises dealing with problems when they are small to prevent them from becoming larger.

questions.question: Interpret the sentence: 'She wore her heart on her sleeve.'

options.B: She wore a special embroidered garment

questions.explanation: 'Wear your heart on your sleeve' means to openly and freely express your emotions without hiding them.

questions.question: What does 'Every cloud has a silver lining' mean?

options.A: Clouds are composed of silver particles
options.B: Every difficult situation has something positive or hopeful in it
options.D: Silver is a very valuable metal

questions.explanation: This saying means that even the worst situations have some positive aspect.

questions.question: Interpret: 'The road to hell is paved with good intentions.'

options.A: Roads to dangerous places are always well-maintained
options.B: Having good intentions is always sufficient
options.C: Good intentions alone are not enough without the right actions
options.D: Hell is a physical place that can be reached by road

questions.explanation: This saying means that good intentions alone do not guarantee good outcomes — action and wisdom are also needed.

questions.question: What does 'barking up the wrong tree' mean?

options.B: Making a mistake about the cause of a problem or target

questions.explanation: 'Barking up the wrong tree' means to pursue a mistaken or misguided course of action.

questions.question: Interpret: 'You can't make an omelette without breaking eggs.'

options.B: You cannot achieve something worthwhile without some sacrifice or difficulty
options.C: Eggs are the most important ingredient in cooking
options.D: Cooking is a very difficult skill

questions.explanation: This saying means that it is impossible to achieve something significant without causing some difficulty or sacrifice.

questions.question: What does 'People who live in glass houses should not throw stones' mean?

options.A: Glass houses are very fragile and should be treated carefully
options.B: People who have faults of their own should not criticize others
options.C: Throwing stones at glass is very dangerous
options.D: Houses made of glass are not suitable for living

questions.explanation: This proverb warns that those who are vulnerable to criticism should not criticize others.

questions.question: Interpret: 'The pen is mightier than the sword.'

options.A: Writing materials are more expensive than weapons
options.B: Communication, knowledge, and writing are more powerful than violence
options.C: Swords are more dangerous than pens
options.D: Writers are better soldiers than warriors

questions.explanation: This saying means that words and ideas have more power and lasting influence than physical force.

questions.question: What does 'A rolling stone gathers no moss' mean?

options.A: Stones should not be rolled downhill
options.B: A person who constantly moves around has no settled base or accumulates nothing
options.C: Moss only grows on stationary stones
options.D: Moving around is beneficial for health

questions.explanation: This proverb suggests that a person who is always on the move and never settles down does not accumulate wealth or strong relationships.

questions.question: Interpret: 'Too many cooks spoil the broth.'

options.A: Cooking meals requires many skilled people
options.B: Having too many people involved in something causes problems
options.C: Broth is a difficult dish to prepare correctly

questions.explanation: This proverb means that when too many people are involved in managing something, it often goes wrong.

questions.question: What does the expression 'Actions speak louder than words' mean?

options.B: What people actually do is more important and revealing than what they say
options.C: Words are not important in communication
options.D: People should speak less and be quieter

questions.explanation: This expression means that what a person does is more significant and credible than what they claim or promise.

questions.question: Interpret: 'Don't bite off more than you can chew.'

options.A: Be very careful when eating large pieces of food
options.B: Do not take on more responsibilities or tasks than you can manage
options.C: Chewing properly is very important for digestion
options.D: Food should always be cut into small pieces before eating

questions.explanation: This saying advises not to take on more work or responsibility than you are capable of handling.

questions.question: What does 'When in Rome, do as the Romans do' mean?

options.A: Everyone should visit the city of Rome
options.B: You should adopt the customs and practices of the place you are visiting
options.C: Romans have the best cultural traditions in the world
options.D: Travel is always an educational experience

questions.explanation: This proverb advises adapting to the customs and traditions of the society you are in.

questions.question: Interpret: 'Let sleeping dogs lie.'

options.A: Dogs should be allowed to sleep undisturbed
options.B: Do not disturb a situation that is currently calm; avoid reopening old disputes
options.C: Dogs are dangerous when they are woken up suddenly
options.D: Allow all animals to rest when they need to

questions.explanation: 'Let sleeping dogs lie' means to avoid interfering with a situation that is currently stable, to avoid causing new trouble.

questions.question: What does 'He is playing devil's advocate' mean?

options.C: He is arguing for the opposing side to stimulate critical thinking
options.D: He is working as a defense lawyer

questions.explanation: 'Playing devil's advocate' means arguing a position one may not personally agree with, to test an argument or explore different viewpoints.

questions.question: Interpret: 'The apple never falls far from the tree.'

options.A: Apples always fall directly beneath the tree
options.B: Children usually resemble or share characteristics with their parents
options.C: Trees produce fruits in their immediate vicinity
options.D: Gardening and agriculture are important skills

questions.explanation: This proverb means that children usually resemble their parents in personality, behaviour, or appearance.

questions.question: What does 'Cut your coat according to your cloth' mean?

options.A: You should learn the art of tailoring and sewing
options.B: Live within your means and do not spend beyond what you have
options.C: Good quality cloth is necessary for fine clothing
options.D: Tailors are important and skilled craftsmen

questions.explanation: This proverb advises people to limit their activities and spending to what they can actually afford.

questions.question: Interpret: 'A bird in hand is worth two in the bush.'

options.A: Birds should be caught and kept in cages
options.B: It is better to have something certain now than to risk losing it for something better that may not happen
options.C: Birds living in bushes are more valuable than those in cages

questions.explanation: This proverb advises appreciating what you have rather than risking it for something uncertain.

questions.question: The prefix 'un-' in the word 'unhappy' means:
questions.explanation: The prefix 'un-' means not or the opposite of. 'Unhappy' therefore means not happy.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct noun form of the verb 'achieve'?
questions.explanation: 'Achievement' is the abstract noun form of 'achieve'. The suffix '-ment' forms nouns from verbs.

questions.question: What is the correct adjective form of the noun 'beauty'?
questions.explanation: 'Beautiful' is the adjective form of 'beauty', formed by adding the suffix '-ful'.

questions.question: The suffix '-tion' in the word 'education' changes the word into a:
questions.explanation: The suffix '-tion' forms nouns from verbs. 'Educate' (verb) becomes 'education' (noun).

questions.question: The prefix 'mis-' in the word 'misunderstand' means:
questions.explanation: The prefix 'mis-' means wrongly or badly. 'Misunderstand' means to understand wrongly.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct adverb form of 'quick'?
questions.explanation: 'Quickly' is the adverb form of 'quick', formed by adding the suffix '-ly'.

questions.question: In the word 'autobiography', the prefix 'auto-' means:
questions.explanation: The prefix 'auto-' comes from Greek meaning 'self'. An autobiography is a story of one's own life.

questions.question: The suffix '-less' in the word 'hopeless' means:
questions.explanation: The suffix '-less' means without. 'Hopeless' means without hope.

questions.question: What is the noun form of the verb 'explain'?
questions.explanation: 'Explanation' is the noun form of 'explain', formed using the suffix '-ation'.

questions.question: Which of the following words uses a prefix meaning 'before'?
questions.explanation: The prefix 'pre-' means before. 'Preview' means to view something before its official release.

questions.question: The word 'bicycle' has the prefix 'bi-' which means:
questions.explanation: The prefix 'bi-' means two. A bicycle has two wheels.

questions.question: Which of the following is the correct plural form of 'phenomenon'?
questions.explanation: 'Phenomena' is the correct Latin/Greek plural of 'phenomenon'. 'Phenomenons' is sometimes used in informal English.

questions.question: What is the abstract noun form of the adjective 'brave'?
questions.explanation: 'Bravery' is the abstract noun formed from the adjective 'brave' using the suffix '-ry'.

questions.question: The suffix '-ous' in the word 'dangerous' changes the noun 'danger' into:
questions.explanation: The suffix '-ous' forms adjectives from nouns. 'Dangerous' is an adjective meaning full of danger.

questions.question: Which of the following words is correctly formed using the suffix '-ment'?
questions.explanation: 'Agreement' is correctly formed from 'agree' + '-ment'. The suffix '-ment' forms nouns from verbs.

questions.question: At the beginning of the novel, what unusual behaviour does Mr. Bepo exhibit at the morning assembly?

options.A: He delivers an angry speech and storms off the stage
options.B: He takes the microphone but breaks down in tears without saying a word
options.C: He announces his resignation to the whole school
options.D: He faints on the podium and has to be carried out

questions.explanation: In Chapter One ('Dusk'), Bepo takes the microphone from the chapel prefect Angel but is overcome with emotion, drops the microphone, and sobs uncontrollably in front of staff and students — saying nothing.

questions.question: Why is Mr. Bepo emotionally distressed in Chapter One of The Lekki Headmaster?

options.A: He has just learnt that a student has died
options.B: He is leaving Nigeria to join his family in the United Kingdom
options.C: He has been sacked by the Managing Director
options.D: He has received news of a bereavement in his family

questions.explanation: As revealed in Chapter Two, Bepo had finally accepted that he must relocate to the UK to join his wife and children after years of resisting the pressure. His emotional breakdown at assembly was caused by the pain of leaving his beloved school and students.

questions.question: How many days of interrogation passed before Mr. Bepo revealed the true reason for his distress in Chapter Two?
questions.explanation: Chapter Two opens: 'After five days of interrogation and frustration, Mr. Bepo opened up.' He had been deflecting questions with 'Thank you. I will be all right' for five days before finally disclosing his plan to relocate to the UK.

questions.question: What was the central grammatical issue that almost cost Mr. Fafore his job in Chapter Six?

options.A: He wrote 'their' instead of 'there' in a student's notebook
options.B: He wrote 'Ade as well as Jide comes early' in a student's note
options.C: He used 'whom' where 'who' was required in a letter to parents
options.D: He misspelt the word 'principal' in a school bulletin

questions.explanation: Parent Mr. Guta complained that Mr. Fafore's use of 'comes' (instead of 'come') in the sentence 'Ade as well as Jide comes early' was a grammatical error. Principal Bepo defended the sentence, explaining that 'as well as' (unlike 'and') takes a singular verb.

questions.question: What was the outcome of the investigation into Mr. Fafore's grammar that almost led to his dismissal?

options.A: Fafore admitted the error and apologised to the parent
options.B: An independent grammar expert was brought in to adjudicate
options.C: Staff checked online and confirmed Fafore and Bepo were correct — the MD was wrong
options.D: The matter was referred to the Ministry of Education for resolution

questions.explanation: The MD directed everyone to check their smartphones. All discovered that 'as well as' with a singular verb is correct in the subjunctive clause structure. The MD, Mrs. Ibidun Gloss, had been wrong, and Fafore was vindicated.

questions.question: What shocking discovery did the MD make when she walked to the piece of land Stardom had acquired near the school?

options.A: She found the land had been sold without her knowledge
options.B: She discovered that ritual objects had been buried on the land
options.C: She found dozens of cars belonging to staff parked there
options.D: She discovered that a rival school was being built on the land

questions.explanation: In Chapter Five ('Snake in the Roof'), the MD discovered that staff were using Stardom's acquired land as a car park. She saw vehicles of various brands — Toyota, Hyundai, Venza, Mazda — and even two buses, some bearing Stardom stickers.

questions.question: What did the accountant Jeremi Amos reveal to the MD about the source of staff funds for buying cars?

options.A: Staff had been stealing from the school's accounts
options.B: Staff were receiving undisclosed salaries from a government scheme
options.C: The school cooperative society had provided loans to the staff
options.D: A wealthy parent had gifted the cars to selected teachers

questions.explanation: The accountant explained that staff had obtained loans from the school cooperative society — which had N95 million in its account with over N50 million loaned out — and some had also raised personal loans from other sources.

questions.question: What decision did the Stardom board of directors take following the revelation about the cooperative society's loans?

options.A: The cooperative society was immediately dissolved
options.B: A cap of N250,000 was placed on individual loans and all requests now required the MD's approval
options.C: All outstanding loans were cancelled and the cooperative was restructured
options.D: Staff were banned from operating the cooperative society

questions.explanation: After the board meeting, it was unanimously decided that no staff member could borrow more than N250,000, all loan requests had to be approved by the MD, and management had to be informed about the cooperative's elections.

questions.question: What did Mr. Bepo witness at 2:51am during his time working at Beesway Group of School?

options.A: A group of men breaking into the school's safe
options.B: The school director and accomplices burying a cow alive on the school grounds
options.C: Students carrying out a midnight cult initiation ceremony
options.D: The director conducting a secret prayer meeting in the dark

questions.explanation: In Chapter Seven ('Ritualists'), Bepo saw seven men (including the Beesway director) and a cow in the darkness. The cow was being pushed into a pit and buried alive — a ritual act that Bepo confronted and was physically attacked for opposing.

questions.question: What offer did the parent Mr. Ogo make to Bepo at Fruitful Future school, which Bepo declined?

options.A: To donate land for a new school building
options.B: To perform a ritual by sprinkling corn at the corners of the school to attract pupils
options.C: To provide free advertising for the school in local newspapers
options.D: To introduce the school to a government bursary scheme

questions.explanation: Mr. Ogo came to Fruitful Future and offered to perform a ritual — sprinkling corn grains at the school's corners — for N35,000, promising it would supernaturally flood the school with pupils. Bepo and his colleague refused.

questions.question: What ultimately happened to Mr. Ogo of Fruitful Future, as Bepo saw on television ten years later?

options.A: He became a successful evangelist and school owner
options.B: He was arrested for murdering a woman who had paid him N9 million to cure her infertility
options.C: He was elected into the local government on a religious platform
options.D: He was exposed as a fraudster who had stolen school funds

questions.explanation: Ten years later, Bepo saw on the news that Mr. Ogo had murdered a civil servant — a woman who had paid him N9 million for a spiritual solution to her infertility. When promised results did not materialise, she threatened him, and he lured and killed her.

questions.question: What caused the suspension of Stardom's democratic prefect election tradition?

options.A: The managing director decided elections were too expensive to organise
options.B: Banky made a vulgar reference to Tosh's father as an 'ex-convict' during his campaign speech
options.C: The school received a government directive banning student elections
options.D: A fight broke out between supporters of two candidates on election day

questions.explanation: During the Speech Day campaign for Social Prefect, SSS2 student Banky said 'Instead of voting for the son of an ex-convict, cast your vote for me,' referring to Tosh's father Chief Didi Ogba. This caused a serious fallout and the democratic tradition was suspended.

questions.question: What was the farewell gift presented to Mr. Bepo by the Stardom management at his send-off?

options.A: A brand new car and a gold wristwatch
options.C: A cash payment of N5 million in naira
options.D: A return ticket to the UK for his entire family

questions.explanation: The MD announced that the gift was not less than $10,000 — the highest Stardom had ever given any departing staff member. It was presented as a domiciliary cheque, not cash, as Bepo was heading to the UK.

questions.question: What happened at the end of the novel on the Monday morning after Bepo's departure?

options.A: A new principal was announced at the morning assembly
options.B: Students boycotted classes in protest at Bepo's departure
options.C: Bepo returned to Stardom, declaring he could not go and would complete his mission
options.D: The MD resigned, saying she could not manage without Bepo

questions.explanation: On the Monday after his departure, as the students gathered at assembly missing Bepo, a shout of 'Principoo!' erupted. Bepo had returned to Stardom, declaring 'I am back! I am here to complete my mission!' — choosing his students and Nigeria over relocation.

questions.question: Why did Bepo miss a flight six years before the events of the final chapter?

options.A: He was stuck in Lagos traffic and arrived after the plane had left
options.B: He misread his British Airways ticket, confusing 11:00am for 1:00pm
options.C: His passport was seized at the airport due to an expired visa
options.D: He arrived on time but was denied boarding for excess luggage

questions.explanation: In Chapter Twelve, Bepo recalled how he once missed a British Airways flight during a visit to his family because he had not carefully checked the ticket — reading '1:00pm' when the flight was actually at '11:00am'. He had to travel the following day after paying a $100 penalty.

questions.question: What is the full real name of the principal referred to throughout the novel as 'Bepo'?
questions.explanation: In Chapter Eleven, the farewell banner reads 'Memorable Farewell for a Most Committed Principal Adebepo Adewale.' The MD confirms: 'Mr Adewale Adebepo, would you kindly step forward.' 'Bepo' is a shortened form of 'Adebepo'.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes the character of Mr. Audu in the novel?

options.A: A serious, disciplined teacher who rarely smiles
options.B: A witty, humorous Fine Arts teacher known for comic remarks and biting observations
options.C: A strict administrator who enforces school rules harshly
options.D: A timid teacher who avoids confrontation with the management

questions.explanation: Mr. Audu, the Fine Arts teacher, is portrayed as the school's comedian — making hilarious observations about economics, the MD, Bepo's relocation, and the Fafore grammar saga. His jokes consistently provide comic relief and lighten tense situations.

questions.question: How is Mrs. Ibidun Gloss, the Managing Director of Stardom Schools, portrayed in the novel?

options.A: As a compassionate leader who always sides with her staff
options.B: As a shrewd, demanding, sometimes impulsive school owner who deeply values the institution
options.C: As a corrupt administrator who embezzles school funds
options.D: As an absentee leader who leaves all decisions to the principal

questions.explanation: Mrs. Gloss is portrayed as a sharp, proud, sometimes rash school proprietor who fiercely guards Stardom's reputation. She acts impulsively (firing Fafore without investigation), but also shows deep appreciation for Bepo and truly values Stardom's brand.

questions.question: What physical trait of Mr. Bepo is frequently mentioned in the novel?

options.A: He is short and stocky with a deep bass voice
options.B: He is tall (six feet two inches), light-skinned, with a powerful voice and prominent eyeballs
options.C: He is heavily built with a thick beard and dark complexion
options.D: He is slim, bespectacled, and constantly well-dressed

questions.explanation: Throughout the novel, Bepo's physical description is emphasised: he is tall (six feet), light-skinned (complexion likened to the yellow salamo ant), has a powerful voice that needed no loudspeaker, and prominent eyeballs. His height made him an effective exam invigilator.

questions.question: What academic discipline did Mr. Bepo study at the University of Benin?
questions.explanation: Chapter Two states that Bepo 'was a trained teacher, having studied English/History Education at the University of Benin.' This training explains his passion for language correctness and history, reflected throughout the novel.

questions.question: What is the name of Mr. Bepo's wife, and what is her profession?
questions.explanation: Bepo's wife is named Seri, and she is a nurse working in the UK. She could reportedly earn up to £10,000 per month (over N17 million). Bepo's children are Nike (at university) and Kike (in high school).

questions.question: What nickname was Mr. Bepo given, and what was its origin?

options.B: 'The Lekki Headmaster' — coined by Mr. Audu, comparing how Bepo resolved staff quarrels to King Oloja's style in the TV drama Village Headmaster
options.C: 'The Weeping Principal' — because of his emotional breakdown at assembly
options.D: 'Oga Tisa' — a humorous Yoruba nickname given by his students

questions.explanation: Mr. Audu coined the nickname 'The Lekki Headmaster' during a staff meeting to settle a quarrel, comparing Bepo's diplomatic conflict-resolution style to King Oloja's in the popular TV drama Village Headmaster. The name stuck even after Bepo became principal at the secondary school.

questions.question: What does the novel reveal about Mr. Fafore's personal housing situation?

options.A: He lived in the school's staff quarters and paid no rent
options.B: He owned his own house in Ifo, Ogun State — having built a two-bedroom flat on half a plot of land
options.C: He rented an apartment in Lekki close to the school
options.D: He shared accommodation with another teacher from Stardom

questions.explanation: Chapter Six narrates Fafore's housing journey: from renting in Ijaye (near Lagos-Ogun boundary) to Sango, before purchasing land in Ifo and building a two-bedroom apartment — even though it had no bath, toilet, or plaster when he first moved in. He woke at 4am daily to arrive early at Stardom.

questions.question: Who was Angel in the context of the novel's opening chapter?

options.A: One of Bepo's two children who had relocated to the UK
options.B: The chapel prefect who handed the microphone to Mr. Bepo at the assembly
options.C: The school nurse who attended to the weeping principal
options.D: A student who gave the pep talk that moved Mr. Bepo to tears

questions.explanation: Angel was the chapel prefect who held the microphone at assembly and handed it to Bepo — who was then expected to give his usual pep talk address following Ikenna Egbu's speech about the Jos excursion.

questions.question: What two characteristics does Mrs. Ibidun Gloss's speech at the farewell reveal about how Bepo got his job at Stardom?

options.A: He was recommended by the Lagos State Ministry of Education
options.B: He was employed directly by the MD's late father, Chief David Aje, after a one-on-one conversation — and then arrived eight days late to resume
options.C: He was poached from a rival school by a generous salary offer
options.D: He applied through a competitive process and scored highest in the interview

questions.explanation: The MD revealed that her late father Chief David Aje employed Bepo directly after seeing his CV and having a conversation. Bepo was expected to resume on September 14 but arrived eight days late — yet Chief Aje patiently waited for him, sensing his exceptional qualities.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST states the major theme of The Lekki Headmaster?

options.A: The dangers of corruption in the Nigerian education system
options.B: The tension between patriotism and self-improvement, as illustrated by the Japa (emigration) phenomenon
options.C: The importance of discipline and punishment in private schools
options.D: The rivalry between Nigeria's public and private school systems

questions.explanation: The novel's central theme is the conflict between love for one's country, profession, and students on one hand, and the material and family pressures pushing Nigerians to emigrate (Japa) on the other. Bepo embodies this dilemma throughout.

questions.question: The theme of dedication to one's vocation is illustrated in the novel primarily through

options.A: The MD's management of Stardom's finances
options.B: Mr. Bepo's 24-year career of passionate teaching and his final decision to return to Stardom
options.C: Mr. Audu's commitment to entertaining the staff with humour
options.D: Sola's determination to succeed in the UK after leaving Nigeria

questions.explanation: Bepo's unwavering dedication to education — staying at Stardom for 24 years, introducing excursions, the pep talk tradition, defending teachers unjustly treated, and ultimately returning — is the novel's clearest illustration of the theme of vocational dedication.

questions.question: In Chapter Nine, Bepo compares the modern Japa movement to which historical event?

options.D: The Biblical Exodus of the Israelites from Egypt

questions.explanation: Standing at the Heritage Slavery Museum in Badagry, Bepo reflects that Africans were once forced into ships as slaves; now they voluntarily flee to work for former 'masters,' selling properties and families for visas. He sees this as a new, self-imposed form of enslavement.

questions.question: The theme of corruption and moral compromise is illustrated in the novel through which of the following incidents?

options.A: Bepo's decision to accept a teaching job in the UK
options.B: The prefect election process at Stardom Schools
options.C: Bepo's use of an agent (Tai) who colluded with immigration officials to expedite his passport renewal
options.D: Mr. Audu's comic remarks about the MD's financial decisions

questions.explanation: In Chapter Ten, Bepo is forced to pay an agent (Tai) N100,000 — above the official N70,000 fee — to get his passport renewed through an informal arrangement. The agent works hand-in-glove with immigration officials, illustrating systemic corruption.

questions.question: The theme of family versus career is most directly illustrated by which plot development?

options.A: The DNA revelation in the Ignatius family
options.B: Mr. Bepo's prolonged resistance to relocating despite his wife and children already living in the UK
options.C: Mr. Fafore's struggle to pay school fees for his children
options.D: The Banky-Tosh rivalry and its parents' involvement

questions.explanation: Bepo loves Stardom and his students deeply, but his wife Seri and children Nike and Kike are in the UK. His marriage is pushed to the brink by his reluctance to join them. This tension between family duty and professional passion is central to the plot.

questions.question: Where is Stardom Schools, the primary setting of The Lekki Headmaster, located?

options.C: The posh heart of Lekki, Lagos

questions.explanation: The novel repeatedly describes Stardom Schools as an A-list school 'located in the posh heart of Lekki' — one of Lagos's most affluent suburbs. This setting explains the wealthy parent population and the high fees charged.

questions.question: In Chapter Seven, Bepo's nighttime encounter with the ritualists takes place at which school?

options.C: Beesway Group of School, on the outskirts of Lagos
options.D: The government school in Warri where Bepo did NYSC

questions.explanation: The nighttime rituals involving the buried cow took place at Beesway Group of School, located on the outskirts of Lagos, where Bepo worked before joining Stardom Schools. He was living in the Beesway Staff Quarters at the time.

questions.question: Badagry, which features prominently in Chapter Nine, is significant in Nigerian history primarily because of its

options.A: Role as Nigeria's first capital city under British colonial rule
options.B: Connection to the Atlantic slave trade and its many slave trade monuments
options.C: Status as the site of Nigeria's first university
options.D: Role as the headquarters of the first Christian mission in Nigeria

questions.explanation: Badagry is described as significant for 'its pioneering contact with Atlantic slave traders.' It is home to the Point of No Return, the Heritage Slavery Museum, the Mobee Royal Family Slave Relics Museum, and the First Storey Building (built by Henry Townsend in 1846).

questions.question: The chapter titles 'Dusk' (Chapter One) and '...Dawn' (Chapter Twelve) are examples of which literary device?

options.A: Oxymoron — placing two contradictory words together
options.B: Symbolism — using the movement from dusk (endings) to dawn (new beginnings) to frame the novel's emotional journey
options.C: Alliteration — the repetition of the same initial sound
options.D: Onomatopoeia — words that imitate natural sounds

questions.explanation: The novel begins at 'Dusk' (the end of something — Bepo's time at Stardom) and closes with '...Dawn' (a new beginning — his return and recommitment). This framing device symbolically underscores the novel's theme of choices and new starts.

questions.question: When the chapter title 'Snake in the Roof' is used in Chapter Five, this is an example of which literary device?

options.C: Metaphor — the cooperative society's large loan fund hidden from the MD is the 'snake' in the roof

questions.explanation: 'Snake in the Roof' is a metaphor, drawn from the Yoruba proverb used by Chief Mrs. Solape Bayo: 'It's like hanging a snake in the roof and going to bed.' The cooperative's dangerously large unsupervised loan fund is the hidden snake threatening Stardom's stability.

questions.question: Mr. Audu's remark — 'Mr. Fafore, as well as the principal, is correct. And the MD, Mrs. Ibidun Gloss, is hereby pardoned, discharged and acquitted' — is an example of

options.A: Irony and satire — using courtroom language humorously to deflate a tense situation
options.B: Dramatic monologue — a character revealing their inner thoughts
options.C: Stream of consciousness — the unfiltered flow of a character's thoughts
options.D: Flashback — returning to an earlier time in the narrative

questions.explanation: Audu uses the solemn language of a court verdict ('pardoned, discharged and acquitted') in a comic context — making a formal declaration about a grammatical dispute. This satirical use of legal register defuses tension and provides comic relief.

questions.question: Bepo's dream during his wait at the airport — where he sees slaves being loaded onto ships and shouts 'Noooo!' — is an example of which narrative technique?

options.C: Dream sequence used as an allegory for the Japa dilemma

questions.explanation: The dream is an allegorical sequence: Bepo watches Africans being forced into a slave ship — mirroring his own impending 'voluntary' emigration. The dream serves as an internal moral reckoning, and his shout of 'Noooo!' foreshadows his ultimate decision to return to Stardom.

questions.question: The Yoruba proverb referenced in Chapter Five — 'It's like hanging a snake in the roof and going to bed' — is an example of

options.B: Proverbial wisdom used to express hidden danger in an everyday context

questions.explanation: The proverb is an example of proverbial wisdom — a traditional saying that condenses a complex truth into a vivid image. Chief Bayo uses it to warn that allowing staff to hold enormous loan funds without oversight is a latent danger, like a live snake hidden in the roof.

questions.question: The narrative technique used to tell Bepo's story, where the author reveals backstory through Bepo's own memories and reflections, is called

options.A: In medias res — starting in the middle of the action
options.B: Flashback (retrospective narrative) — inserting past events into the present timeline
options.C: Epistolary narrative — telling the story through letters
options.D: Second-person narrative — addressing the reader as 'you'

questions.explanation: Throughout the novel, the author uses flashbacks — Bepo recalls his time at Beesway, the Fruitful Future experience, the electricity tariff incident, Sola's migration stories — to fill in his character's history within the present narrative.

questions.question: Which of the following BEST describes the author's narrative style in The Lekki Headmaster?

options.A: Minimalist prose with short, sparse sentences
options.B: A rich blend of humour, social commentary, proverbs, and vivid Nigerian socio-cultural detail
options.C: Highly poetic language with extensive use of verse within the chapters
options.D: A purely formal academic style devoid of humour

questions.explanation: Garba's style is a lively mix of humour (through characters like Audu), social criticism (of Japa, corruption, educational decay), Yoruba and other Nigerian proverbs, code-switching, and precise cultural references — creating an authentically Nigerian narrative voice.

questions.question: According to Mr. Bepo's explanation in Chapter Six, what grammatical rule explains why 'Ade as well as Jide comes early' is correct?

options.A: Conjunctions like 'as well as' always take a plural verb in Standard English
options.B: When 'as well as', 'together with', or 'alongside' are used, the verb agrees with the first subject — giving a singular verb with 's'
options.C: The verb 'comes' is used because both Ade and Jide are male names
options.D: This is a case of American English differing from British English usage

questions.explanation: Bepo explains: 'When you use 'and', the verb that follows is 'come'. But when you use 'as well as', 'together with', 'alongside', etc., we go for the singular verb with 's'. The clause is in the subjunctive mood and does not align with normal grammar rules.'

questions.question: In Chapter Seven, the director of Beesway School dismisses Bepo's correction about 'Group of School' by saying, 'Mr. Bepo, the Englisher, let's leave the matter as it is.' What grammatical error does this statement itself contain?

options.A: 'Let's' should be 'let us' in formal speech
options.B: 'Englisher' is not a standard English word and should be 'English teacher' or 'English expert'

questions.explanation: Bepo notices but suppresses the urge to correct the director's use of 'Englisher.' Ironically, Bepo then points out that 'Englisher' actually exists in dictionaries (referring to a translator from another language into English), further embarrassing the director.

questions.question: The title of Chapter Six, 'Ade as Well as Jide COMES vs. COME', suggests the chapter is primarily concerned with which literary and linguistic theme?

options.A: The conflict between two students named Ade and Jide
options.B: Subject-verb agreement and the way professional integrity can hinge on linguistic knowledge
options.C: The need for a national standard of spoken English in Nigerian schools
options.D: The differences between English as spoken in the UK and in Nigeria

questions.explanation: The chapter uses a grammar dispute as its central dramatic device to explore professional integrity, the dangers of hasty judgement, and the critical importance of linguistic knowledge — particularly for someone who calls themselves an English teacher.

questions.question: The novel portrays the Japa phenomenon (mass emigration from Nigeria) as

options.A: An entirely positive development that Nigeria should encourage
options.B: A morally simple choice that any rational person would make
options.C: A complex, sometimes painful decision driven by economic hardship, family pressure, and disappointment with Nigerian systems
options.D: A purely political act of protest against the Nigerian government

questions.explanation: The novel presents Japa as deeply ambivalent — some emigrants thrive (Sola, Riike), others struggle (Hope, Jare). Bepo himself is torn between the economic logic of emigration and his passionate love for his students and his country, illustrating the issue's complexity.

questions.question: What does the novel suggest about the housing situation of teachers in Lagos?

options.A: Teachers in Lagos enjoy subsidised accommodation provided by the government
options.B: Most teachers cannot afford the high rents in Lekki and commute long distances from cheaper areas like Ifo and Iyana Ipaja
options.C: Teachers at private schools like Stardom are provided with comfortable staff quarters on campus
options.D: Teachers in Lagos earn enough to rent comfortable flats near their schools

questions.explanation: Both Fafore (who lives in Ifo, Ogun State) and other teachers wake as early as 4:00am to commute to Lekki. A two-bedroom flat in Lekki costs over N2 million, with landlords demanding two years' upfront — beyond reach on a teacher's salary of N175,000-N400,000.

questions.question: The DNA revelation in the Ignatius family in Chapter Four primarily comments on which social issue?

options.B: The devastating impact of unresolved marital conflicts, secrets, and infidelity on family plans, especially those involving relocation
options.C: The inadequacy of Nigeria's identity documentation system
options.D: The challenges of single parenting in modern Nigeria

questions.explanation: The DNA test that revealed Mr. Ignatius was not Favour's biological father exposed a hidden marital crisis. Combined with the planned relocation to the UK, it shattered the family — illustrating how personal secrets can derail even the most carefully laid life plans.

questions.question: What criticism of Nigeria's bureaucratic system does Chapter Ten ('Passport Pains') make?

options.A: That the immigration system works perfectly for those willing to follow official channels
options.B: That corruption has so infiltrated the passport renewal process that the official online system is deliberately made dysfunctional to push citizens toward bribe-paying agents
options.C: That the government has closed all passport offices in Lagos due to overcrowding
options.D: That only Nigerians with overseas connections can renew their passports successfully

questions.explanation: Chapter Ten exposes that passport renewal requires paying unofficial agents (like Tai) who collude with immigration staff. The official online platform is widely suspected of being deliberately made erratic to force citizens into the corrupt informal system.

questions.question: The novel begins with Mr. Bepo's breakdown at assembly and ends with his return to Stardom. This narrative structure is an example of

options.A: A linear, chronological plot with no flashbacks
options.B: A circular narrative structure — beginning and ending at the same location (Stardom's assembly ground), bookending the story's emotional journey
options.C: An episodic structure with entirely unrelated chapters
options.D: A tragic structure ending in the protagonist's defeat

questions.explanation: The novel opens at a morning assembly with tears and confusion (dusk) and ends at a morning assembly with joy and homecoming (dawn). This circular structure reinforces the thematic message that Bepo's true home is Stardom, and that he has come full circle.

questions.question: The author's use of multiple episodes (the Fafore grammar affair, the cooperative saga, the passport ordeal, the migration tales) rather than a single linear plot serves to

options.A: Confuse the reader and obscure the novel's central message
options.B: Present a panoramic, socially rich picture of Nigerian institutional life and the dilemmas of emigration
options.C: Demonstrate the author's inability to maintain a single storyline
options.D: Limit the novel to readers who are already familiar with Nigerian culture

questions.explanation: The episodic structure allows Garba to comment on multiple aspects of Nigerian life — educational management, corruption, housing, migration, grammar, and cultural identity — while maintaining Bepo's dilemma as the unifying thread, creating a socially comprehensive novel.

questions.question: The dedication of The Lekki Headmaster reads: 'This book is dedicated to all teachers committed to the cause of sound education. You are truly the real builders of the nation!' This dedication suggests the novel's overarching message is

options.A: That teachers should emigrate to countries that pay them better
options.B: That dedicated teachers are the foundation of national development and deserve greater recognition
options.C: That only private school teachers deserve respect and praise
options.D: That the Nigerian government adequately rewards its teachers

questions.explanation: The dedication positions the novel as a tribute to dedicated teachers — the 'real builders of the nation.' The entire story of Bepo's 24-year selfless commitment to Stardom, and his ultimate decision to return, is the author's literary embodiment of this dedication.
